According to state climatological records, the year 1996 was the wettest on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The state-wide average was 59.97 inches, about 14.5 inches above normal.  The previous record was 58.85 inches set in 1975.   Precipitation amounts on a county average ranged from 56.5 inches in Cumberland County to 65.3 inches in Morris County.  The wettest county was  Bergen where a county weighed average of 70.7 inches fell. It was the wettest year on record at Lambertville (Hunterdon County) as 69.82 inches of precipitation fell.  It was also the wettest year on record in Philadelphia as 56.47 inches of precipitation fell.  Other yearly totals included 64.53 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 63.21 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 61.52 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County/3rd wettest), 60.53 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 57.58 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 56.38 inches in Cape May (3rd wettest) and 50.46 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona.  On a county average, the yearly precipitation total ranged from 12 to 21 inches above normal (It was higher in Northeast New Jersey).

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina and Georgia the morning of the 7th and moved quickly northeast passing over the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon of the 7th, just east of the New Jersey coast during the evening of the 7th and near Portland, Maine on the morning of the 8th.  The onshore flow accompanying the low pressure system kept the tide from receding overnight and the ensuing high tide the morning of the 8th was about 2 feet above normal south of Manasquan Inlet.  The high tide at Atlantic City reached 6.6 feet above mean low water.  Tides were about 2.5 feet above normal along Coastal Monmouth County.  Only minor tidal flooding was reported along New Jersey's coast.

Rain, which fell from December 1st into the morning of the 2nd became heavy at times during the early morning of the 2nd.  This  caused flooding along the Assunpink Creek within Mercer County.  The Assunpink Creek in Trenton, reached its 7 foot flood stage at 430 a.m. EST on the 2nd, crested at 8.3 feet at 830 a.m. EST on the 2nd and was back within bankfull at 1130 a.m. EST on the 2nd.  Storm totals included 2.4 inches in Washington's Crossing and 1.85 inches in Hightstown.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.

The combination of heavy rain and higher than normal tides helped cause flooding along the Cooper River in Camden County.  Admiral Wilson Boulevard along tidal sections of the Cooper River in Camden was closed.  Farther upstream, at Haddonfield, the Cooper River was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 8 a.m. through 1115 a.m. EST on the 6th.  It crested at 2.87 feet at 915 a.m. EST on the 6th.  Storm totals from Camden County included 1.5 inches in Pennsauken, 1.39 inches in Somerdale and 1.37 inches in Cherry Hill.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina the afternoon of the 5th.  As it quickly intensified and moved northeast (It passed just east of the New Jersey coast the morning of the 6th.), the precipitation associated with it changed from rain to snow across the higher terrain of Sussex County during the early morning  of the 6th.  As the storm continued to intensify during the morning of the 6th, so did the precipitation.  The heaviest snow fell between about 7 a.m. and 11 a.m EST.  The snow was very elevation dependent.  While the higher terrain locations were receiving heavy snow, considerably less fell in the valleys where precipitation fell as or mixed with rain longer.  Accumulations included 11.5 inches at High Point, 7 inches in Sparta, and only one inch in Newton.

A low pressure system developed off the Virginia Coast during Friday, December 13th.  It rapidly intensified as it moved slowly east out to sea during Saturday, December 14th.This low produced heavy rain across the area, which briefly changed to snow before ending.Here are selected rainfall amounts for:Passaic County:  1.20 inches at Ringwood and 1.36 inches at Wayne.Bergen County:  from 1.12 inches at Ridgewood to 1.91 inches at Franklin Lakes.Essex County:  from 1.34 inches at Newark Airport to 1.86 inches at Verona.Union County:  2.63 inches at Canoe Brook.

December 1996 was the wettest December on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The statewide average of 7.9 inches surpassed the previous record of 7.29 inches set in 1973.  Precipitation amounts ranged from about 3.5 inches above normal in Middlesex County to 5.7 inches above normal in Warren County.  A new December precipitation record was set at nearby Philadelphia International Airport where 8.48 inches fell.  December 1996 was the second wettest on record at the Atlantic City International Airport (in Pomona) where 7.23 inches of precipitation fell.  December 1996 was the 8th wettest on record at the Marina in Atlantic City as 6.87 inches of rain fell.  County average rainfall totals ranged from 7.4 inches in Middlesex County to 9.2 inches in Hunterdon, Morris and Warren Counties.

A slow moving cold front, coupled with a low pressure system that developed over West Virginia on the 1st and moved just northwest of New Jersey on the 2nd brought rain to the area from the 1st through the morning of the 2nd.  The rain became heavy at times during the early morning of the 2nd. This led to flooding in the Passaic and Raritan Basins.In Morris County, the Rockaway River both above and below the Boonton Reservoir exceeded their 5 foot flood stage.  Below the reservoir, the Rockaway was above flood stage from 4 a.m. EST on the 2nd through 2 a.m. EST on the 4th.  It crested at 6.3 feet at 8 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  Above the reservoir, the Rockaway was above flood stage from 1030 a.m. through 9 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  It crested at 5.2 feet at 315 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  The Passaic River at Pine Brook was above its estimated 19 foot flood stage from 2 a.m. EST on the 2nd through 830 p.m. EST on the 5th.  It crested at 19.4 feet at 8 p.m. EST on the 3rd.  Storm totals included 3.27 inches in Long Valley and 3.23 inches in Boonton.  No serious injuries or damage were reported. In Somerset County,  the Millstone River at Blackwells Mills was above its 9 foot flood stage from 11 a.m. EST on the 2nd through 10 a.m. EST on the 3rd.  It crested at 10.5 feet at 930 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  The Raritan River at Manville reached its 12 foot flood stage at 630 a.m. EST on the 2nd, crested at 15.7 feet at 515 p.m. EST on the 2nd and was back within bankfull at 4 a.m. EST on the 3rd.  Farther downstream, the Raritan at Bound Brook was above its 26 foot flood stage from 1130 a.m. EST on the 2nd through 730 a.m. EST on the 3rd.  It crested at 28.9 feet at 815 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  The flooding caused the closure of six roadways in Bedminster, Branchburg (three roads), Bridgewater, Franklin,  Hillsborough and Montgomery.  At least one home in Branchburg on New Jersey State Route 28 was damaged by flooding along the North Branch of the Raritan River.  Storm totals included 3.48 inches in Pottersville and 2.23 inches in Bridgewater.   In Hunterdon County, the South Branch of the Raritan River reached its 8 foot flood stage at 6 a.m. EST on the 2nd, crested at 9.6 feet at 745 a.m. EST on the 2nd and was back within bankfull at 1045 a.m. EST on the 2nd.  Storm totals included 3.37 inches in Califon, 2.68 inches in Lambertville and 2.63 inches in Wertsville.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.

Farther downstream, the Raritan at Bound Brook was above its 26 foot flood stage from 1130 a.m. EST on the 2nd through 730 a.m. EST on the 3rd.  It crested at 28.9 feet at 815 p.m. EST on the 2nd.  The flooding caused the closure of six roadways in Bedminster, Branchburg (three roads), Bridgewater, Franklin,  Hillsborough and Montgomery.  At least one home in Branchburg on New Jersey State Route 28 was damaged by flooding along the North Branch of the Raritan River.  Storm totals included 3.48 inches in Pottersville and 2.23 inches in Bridgewater.   In Hunterdon County, the South Branch of the Raritan River reached its 8 foot flood stage at 6 a.m. EST on the 2nd, crested at 9.6 feet at 745 a.m. EST on the 2nd and was back within bankfull at 1045 a.m. EST on the 2nd.  Storm totals included 3.37 inches in Califon, 2.68 inches in Lambertville and 2.63 inches in Wertsville.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.

According to state climatological records, the year 1996 was the wettest on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The state-wide average was 59.97 inches, about 14.5 inches above normal.  The previous record was 58.85 inches set in 1975.   Precipitation amounts on a county average ranged from 56.5 inches in Cumberland County to 65.3 inches in Morris County.  The wettest county was  Bergen where a county weighed average of 70.7 inches fell. It was the wettest year on record at Lambertville (Hunterdon County) as 69.82 inches of precipitation fell.  It was also the wettest year on record in Philadelphia as 56.47 inches of precipitation fell.  Other yearly totals included 64.53 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 63.21 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 61.52 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County/3rd wettest), 60.53 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 57.58 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 56.38 inches in Cape May (3rd wettest) and 50.46 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona.  On a county average, the yearly precipitation total ranged from 12 to 21 inches above normal (It was higher in Northeast New Jersey).

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina the afternoon of the 5th.  As it quickly intensified and moved northeast (It passed just east of the New Jersey coast the morning of the 6th.), heavy rain spread across New Jersey after midnight on the 6th.  Storm totals averaged around 1.50 inches across the area and produced some urban and poor drainage flooding.  This was the second soaking precipitation event within the week and month.  Rainfall amounts were less than one inch farther southeast in the state.  The rain changed briefly to snow before ending across western parts of the state.  Precipitation totals included 1.74 inches in Reigelsville (Hunterdon County), 1.66 inches in Freehold (Monmouth County), 1.62 inches at the McGuire AFB, 1.5 inches in Seabrook (Cumberland County), 1.48 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 1.44 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 1.34 inches in Washington's Crossing (Mercer County), 1.29 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County) and 1.24 inches in Belvidere (Warren County).

December 1996 was the wettest December on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The statewide average of 7.9 inches surpassed the previous record of 7.29 inches set in 1973.  Precipitation amounts ranged from about 3.5 inches above normal in Middlesex County to 5.7 inches above normal in Warren County.  A new December precipitation record was set at nearby Philadelphia International Airport where 8.48 inches fell.  December 1996 was the second wettest on record at the Atlantic City International Airport (in Pomona) where 7.23 inches of precipitation fell.  December 1996 was the 8th wettest on record at the Marina in Atlantic City as 6.87 inches of rain fell.  County average rainfall totals ranged from 7.4 inches in Middlesex County to 9.2 inches in Hunterdon, Morris and Warren Counties.

A rapidly deepening low pressure system located just east of Seaside Heights at 7 a.m. EST the morning of the 6th produced high winds across Southeast New Jersey.  Wind gusts reached 61 mph at Seaside Park.  A wind gust in excess of 60 mph was also reported in Hammonton.  Peak gusts at the Atlantic City International Airport reached 52 mph and 47 mph at the Marina in Atlantic City.  Most of the gusts occurred with west winds after the low pressure system had passed.  No significant damage was reported.

The combination of very strong east winds from a rapidly intensifying low pressure system coniciding with the time of astronomical high tide caused some minor tidal flooding during the early morning of the 6th across extreme Southeast New Jersey.  Several inches of water accumulated on the Black Horse Pike (United States Routes 40 and 322) near Atlantic City.  Some minor tidal flooding also occurred in the flood prone areas of Cape May County.

High winds pulled down trees and power lines during the late afternoon and early evening of the 1st in Sussex and Warren Counties.  About 2,000 homes lost power.  Damage occurred in Warren County in Columbia and within White and Liberty Townships.  Damage was reported in Sussex County in Fredon, Hampton, Montague, Sparta, Stillwater and Walpack.

A slow moving low pressure system moved from the central Ohio Valley the morning of the 12th to Williamsburg, Virginia the morning of the 13th, to about 100 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 14th, then drifted southeast and was about 300 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 15th.  This produced about 48 hours of continuous rain from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th.  Rain became heavy at times during the night of the 13th through the morning of the 14th.  Rain mixed with snow at times during the morning of the 14th over some of the higher terrain of Northwest New Jersey.  Accumulation averaged around two inches in Wantage, Newton and Vernon.  Storm totals averaged between one and four inches with the lowest amounts in Sussex County and the highest amounts in the southwest part of the state.   Since the rain was spread over an extended period of time, flooding was mainly confined to urban and poor drainage areas.  Basement flooding did occur, with the highest reports in Burlington County.  The heavy rain also damaged a bridge over the Cranbury Creek in Plainsboro (Middlesex County).  Precipitation totals included 4.28 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County),  3.89 inches in Verga (Gloucester County), 2.61 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), 2.49 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.00 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.93 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 1.71 inches within Atlantic City, 1.58 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.37 inches in Newton (Sussex County).

The combination of some very light snow (for most the first snow of the season), road and air temperatures below freezing and heavy  traffic volume because of Thanksgiving Day caused roadways to become extremely slippery and hazardous, especially on bridges and overpasses.  Accumulations were less than an inch and the snow was over by 6 p.m. EST.  But that was enough to cause accidents across the area.  The worst conditions were over the western parts of the area.  They were even worse in Northern Delaware and Eastern Pennsylvania.  Interstate 78 in Hunterdon County had many accidents.  The Commodore Barry Bridge was closed due to icing until 9 p.m. EST.

A low pressure system over the Ohio Valley early Sunday morning on December 1st, intensified as it moved northeast across Eastern Canada during early Monday morning.  A strong cold front that extended southwest from this low, swept across the local region early Monday morning.Lines of thunderstorms formed in the moist unstable airmass ahead of the cold front.  These storms produced numerous wind gusts from 58 MPH in East Orange of Essex County to 65 MPH at both Garwood and Berkeley Heights in Union County.Thunderstorms also caused torrential rainfall.  The Ramapo River at Mahwah crested at 8.10 feet, just above its' 8.00 foot flood stage.  Small stream, street, and poor drainage flooding was widespread over Union, Passaic, and Bergen Counties.Occasional rain began across the region during Saturday afternoon, November 30th.  Here are selected rainfall event totals:For Passaic County:  from 1.82 inches at Wanaque to 4.51 inches at Ringwood.For Bergen County:  from 2.00 inches at Franklin Lakes to 4.38 inches at Saddle River.For Essex County:  from 1.83 inches at Newark Airport to 3.60 inches at Verona.For Hudson County:  from 1.59 inches at Jersey City to 2.20 inches at Harrison.For Union County:  from 2.15 inches at Canoe Brook to 2.61 inches at Elizabeth.

Other yearly totals included 64.53 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 63.21 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 61.52 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County/3rd wettest), 60.53 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 57.58 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 56.38 inches in Cape May (3rd wettest) and 50.46 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona.  On a county average, the yearly precipitation total ranged from 12 to 21 inches above normal (It was higher in Northeast New Jersey).

The combination of some very light snow (for most the first snow of the season), road and air temperatures below freezing and heavy  traffic volume because of Thanksgiving Day caused roadways to become extremely slippery and hazardous, especially on bridges and overpasses.  Accumulations were less than an inch and the snow was over by 6 p.m. EST.  But that was enough to cause accidents across the area.  The worst conditions were over the western parts of the area.  They were even worse in Northern Delaware and Eastern Pennsylvania.  Interstate 78 in Hunterdon County had many accidents.  The Commodore Barry Bridge was closed due to icing until 9 p.m. EST.
Very strong winds preceded a cold front that moved through the area during the evening of the 8th.  Downed trees were reported in Green Township.
Very strong winds preceded and accompanied a cold front that moved through the area during the evening of the 8th.  Trees and wires were knocked down in Woodstown.

A rapidly deepening low pressure system located just east of Seaside Heights at 7 a.m. EST the morning of the 6th produced high winds across Southeast New Jersey.  Wind gusts reached 61 mph at Seaside Park.  A wind gust in excess of 60 mph was also reported in Hammonton.  Peak gusts at the Atlantic City International Airport reached 52 mph and 47 mph at the Marina in Atlantic City.  Most of the gusts occurred with west winds after the low pressure system had passed.  No significant damage was reported.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina and Georgia the morning of the 7th and moved quickly northeast passing over the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon of the 7th, just east of the New Jersey during the evening of the 7th and near Portland Maine on the morning of the 8th.  For the third time this month at least an inch of rain (heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening) fell across most of Western New Jersey.  Some urban and poor drainage flooding occurred,  but no serious injuries or damage were reported.  Storm totals included 1.58 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.50 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 1.37 inches in Millville (Cumberland County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 1.33 inches within Atlantic City, 1.28 inches in Verga (Gloucester County), 1.20 inches in Atsion (Burlington County), 1.19 inches in Cherry Hill (Camden County) and Mount Holly (Burlington County) and 1.18 inches in Somerdale (Camden County).

December 1996 was the wettest December on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The statewide average of 7.9 inches surpassed the previous record of 7.29 inches set in 1973.  Precipitation amounts ranged from about 3.5 inches above normal in Middlesex County to 5.7 inches above normal in Warren County.  A new December precipitation record was set at nearby Philadelphia International Airport where 8.48 inches fell.  December 1996 was the second wettest on record at the Atlantic City International Airport (in Pomona) where 7.23 inches of precipitation fell.  December 1996 was the 8th wettest on record at the Marina in Atlantic City as 6.87 inches of rain fell.  County average rainfall totals ranged from 7.4 inches in Middlesex County to 9.2 inches in Hunterdon, Morris and Warren Counties.

A slow moving low pressure system moved from the central Ohio Valley the morning of the 12th to Williamsburg, Virginia the morning of the 13th, to about 100 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 14th, then drifted southeast and was about 300 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 15th.  This produced about 48 hours of continuous rain from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th.  Rain became heavy at times during the night of the 13th through the morning of the 14th.  Rain mixed with snow at times during the morning of the 14th over some of the higher terrain of Northwest New Jersey.  Accumulation averaged around two inches in Wantage, Newton and Vernon.  Storm totals averaged between one and four inches with the lowest amounts in Sussex County and the highest amounts in the southwest part of the state.   Since the rain was spread over an extended period of time, flooding was mainly confined to urban and poor drainage areas.  Basement flooding did occur, with the highest reports in Burlington County.  The heavy rain also damaged a bridge over the Cranbury Creek in Plainsboro (Middlesex County).  Precipitation totals included 4.28 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County),  3.89 inches in Verga (Gloucester County), 2.61 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), 2.49 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.00 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.93 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 1.71 inches within Atlantic City, 1.58 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.37 inches in Newton (Sussex County).

A slow moving cold front, coupled with a low pressure system that developed over West Virginia on the 1st and moved just northwest of New Jersey on the 2nd brought rain to the area from the 1st through the morning of the 2nd.  The rain was the heaviest during the early morning of the 2nd and caused some urban and poor drainage flooding.  River flooding was mainly confined to the Passaic and Raritan Basins.  Storm totals averaged between 1.5 and 2.5 inches with higher amounts in the northwest part of the state and lower amounts along the southeast shore.  Storm totals included 3.17 inches in Sparta (Sussex County), 2.89 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.53 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 2.19 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 1.76 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County), 1.75 inches in Brick Township (Ocean County), 1.68 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.61 inches in Millville (Cumberland County), 1.6 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and 1.06 inches within Atlantic City.

Heavy rain associated with a low pressure system off the New Jersey coast during the evening of the 7th caused the flooding of some of the smaller streams in Somerset County.  The flooding reached the larger rivers within the Raritan Basin during the morning of the 8th.  Storm totals included 1.26 inches in Pottersville.  This was already the third time this month that a precipitation event produced more than an inch of rain across the county.

Heavy rain of around an inch to an inch and a half combined with already saturated soil from the previous rains this month once again caused flooding along the Millstone and Raritan Rivers.  The Raritan River at Manville reached its 12 foot flood stage at 3 a.m. EST on the 8th, crested at 12.8 feet at 645 a.m. EST on the 8th and was back within its banks at 1030 a.m. EST.  At Bound Brook, the Raritan River was above its 26 foot flood stage from 7 a.m. until 230 p.m. EST on the 8th.  It crested at 26.7 feet at 1030 a.m. EST on the 8th.  The Millstone River at Blackwells Mills reached its 9 foot flood stage at 5 a.m. EST on the 8th, crested at 9.5 feet at 1 p.m. EST on the 8th and was back within bankfull at 10 p.m. EST.  No serious damage was reported.

A slow moving low pressure system, that moved from Williamsburg Virginia the morning of the 13th to about 100 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 14th and then drifted southeast to about 300 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 15th, and a high pressure system anchored over Quebec Province Canada produced a costant onshore flow for a couple of days.  This was augmented by the astronomical spring tides around the new moon.  Moderate tidal flooding occurred the morning of the 13th.  Minor tidal flooding persisted mainly during the higher morning high tides through the 15th.  The greatest tidal departures averaged about 2.5 feet above normal.  The high tides at Atlantic City reached 7.5 feet above mean low water the morning of the 13th, 7.1 feet above mean low water the morning of the 14th and 6.8 feet above mean low water the morning of the 15th.  The high tides at the Cape May Ferry Terminal reached 8.0 feet above mean low water the morning of the 13th, 7.3 feet above mean low water the morning of the 14th and 7.4 feet above mean low water the morning of the 15th.  The persistent east to northeast flow caused most of the moderate flooding the morning of the 13th to occur around the back bays as the water was unable to escape between high tide cycles. In Monmouth County, tidal flooding and road closures occurred around the Shrewsbury River in Sea Bright.  In Ocean County, back bay flooding inundated Ship Bottom's Central Avenue from 26th through 29th Streets.  In Atlantic County, a one mile stretch of the Black Horse Pike (U.S. Routes 40 and 322) was closed between Atlantic City and Pleasantville the morning of the 13th.  The westbound lanes were reopened that afternoon,  but the eastbound lanes remained closed all day. In Absecon, flooding forced the closure of U.S. Route 9 and Shore Road and caused three-hour-long traffic backups.  Other street closures due to flooding occurred in Atlantic City (the west side), Brigantine, Margate and Ventnor.    In Cape May County, tidal flooding forced the closure of New Jersey State Route 47 in Wildwood as well as Park Boulevard. In North Wildwood Spruce Avenue and the west end of town were flooded.  Five motorists were rescued from their submerged vehicles. Eight blocks in Ocean City were closed due to flooding.  The tidal flooding forced open manhole covers on 9th Street.  Dune Road was closed in Avalon.  Three roads were barricaded and closed through Saturday morning in Cape May City.  In West Cape May, the Cape Island Creek overflowed forcing the closure of the Leaming/Elmira Street Bridge.  Low lying flooding was reported around Schellenger's Landing in Lower Township.  Beach erosion occurred at the Cape May Point State Park and snow fencing was destroyed in Stone Harbor.   No significant property damage was reported.

A low pressure system over the Ohio Valley early Sunday morning on December 1st, intensified as it moved northeast across Eastern Canada during early Monday morning.  A strong cold front that extended southwest from this low, swept across the local region early Monday morning.Lines of thunderstorms formed in the moist unstable airmass ahead of the cold front.  These storms produced numerous wind gusts from 58 MPH in East Orange of Essex County to 65 MPH at both Garwood and Berkeley Heights in Union County.Thunderstorms also caused torrential rainfall.  The Ramapo River at Mahwah crested at 8.10 feet, just above its' 8.00 foot flood stage.  Small stream, street, and poor drainage flooding was widespread over Union, Passaic, and Bergen Counties.Occasional rain began across the region during Saturday afternoon, November 30th.  Here are selected rainfall event totals:For Passaic County:  from 1.82 inches at Wanaque to 4.51 inches at Ringwood.For Bergen County:  from 2.00 inches at Franklin Lakes to 4.38 inches at Saddle River.For Essex County:  from 1.83 inches at Newark Airport to 3.60 inches at Verona.For Hudson County:  from 1.59 inches at Jersey City to 2.20 inches at Harrison.For Union County:  from 2.15 inches at Canoe Brook to 2.61 inches at Elizabeth.

Heavy rain, which fell from December 1st into the morning of the 2nd caused flooding on some of the smaller streams as well as urban flooding in the county.  The heaviest rain fell after midnight on the 2nd.  Numerous roads were closed due to small stream and roadway flooding including the Netcong Circle.  The small stream flooding and heavy rain led to flooding along the Rockaway and Passaic Rivers.  Storm totals included 3.27 inches in Long Valley and 3.23 inches in Boonton.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina and Georgia the morning of the 7th and moved quickly northeast passing over the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon of the 7th, just east of the New Jersey coast during the evening of the 7th and near Portland, Maine on the morning of the 8th.  In a similar occurrence to what transpired during the morning of the 6th, precipitation spread across Sussex County by early afternoon on the 7th.  Precipitation type became elevation dependent.  While a mixture of rain and snow fell in the valleys, more heavy wet snow fell over the higher terrain.  The heaviest snow fell during the evening of the 7th.  Snowfall accumulations averaged around 2 inches in the valleys and as high as around 7 inches over the higher terrain in the northwest part of the county.  Representative accumulations included 7 inches at High Point and Wantage, 5 inches in Hainesville and 2 inches in Newton.

A slow moving low pressure system moved from the central Ohio Valley the morning of the 12th to Williamsburg, Virginia the morning of the 13th, to about 100 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 14th, then drifted southeast and was about 300 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 15th.  This produced about 48 hours of continuous rain from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th.  Rain became heavy at times during the night of the 13th through the morning of the 14th.  Rain mixed with snow at times during the morning of the 14th over some of the higher terrain of Northwest New Jersey.  Accumulation averaged around two inches in Wantage, Newton and Vernon.  Storm totals averaged between one and four inches with the lowest amounts in Sussex County and the highest amounts in the southwest part of the state.   Since the rain was spread over an extended period of time, flooding was mainly confined to urban and poor drainage areas.  Basement flooding did occur, with the highest reports in Burlington County.  The heavy rain also damaged a bridge over the Cranbury Creek in Plainsboro (Middlesex County).  Precipitation totals included 4.28 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County),  3.89 inches in Verga (Gloucester County), 2.61 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), 2.49 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.00 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.93 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 1.71 inches within Atlantic City, 1.58 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.37 inches in Newton (Sussex County).

According to state climatological records, the year 1996 was the wettest on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The state-wide average was 59.97 inches, about 14.5 inches above normal.  The previous record was 58.85 inches set in 1975.   Precipitation amounts on a county average ranged from 56.5 inches in Cumberland County to 65.3 inches in Morris County.  The wettest county was  Bergen where a county weighed average of 70.7 inches fell. It was the wettest year on record at Lambertville (Hunterdon County) as 69.82 inches of precipitation fell.  It was also the wettest year on record in Philadelphia as 56.47 inches of precipitation fell.  Other yearly totals included 64.53 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 63.21 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 61.52 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County/3rd wettest), 60.53 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 57.58 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 56.38 inches in Cape May (3rd wettest) and 50.46 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona.  On a county average, the yearly precipitation total ranged from 12 to 21 inches above normal (It was higher in Northeast New Jersey).

A low pressure system formed off the South Carolina Coastal waters on the morning of the 1st.  It moved north during the next two days and was about 200 miles east of Cape Hatteras at dawn on the 2nd and about 150 miles east of Atlantic City during the afternoon.  Its storm track was far enough offshore to place the axis of heavy snow across New Jersey.  Very little snow fell farther west.Snow began just after midnight on the 2nd and fell at a heavy clip almost from the start.  The heavy snow was over by noon.  Accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were fairly common throughout the southern half of the state.  Around 6 inches of snow fell in Mercer, Middlesex and Monmouth Counties; 4 to 5 inches in Morris, Hunterdon and Somerset Counties and 2 to 4 inches across Warren and Sussex Counties.  Some representative accumulations included 9 inches at McGuire AFB and Tabernacle, 8 inches in Mount Holly, Toms River and Washington Township (Gloucester County), 7 inches in Marlboro and Cherry Hill and 6 inches in Pilesgrove, Iselin, Atlantic City and Cape May City.Because this snow fell on a Saturday morning,  the number of accidents was held down.  There were two separate crashes involving buses with snow as a contributing factor.  In Old Bridge Township at 520 a.m., a 65-year-old man passsenger was killed on a Gray Line Tour Bus as it slid and overturned on the Garden State Parkway.  Thirty-four other people were injured.  The parkway was closed for seven hours between the Asbury Park and Raritan Toll Plazas.  At 8 a.m. in Hammonton a Greyhound Bus coming from Atlantic City collided with a Chevrolet Monte Carlo that was spinning out of control on the Atlantic City Expressway.  Forty-six passengers were injured.

A low pressure system over the Ohio Valley early Sunday morning on December 1st, intensified as it moved northeast across Eastern Canada during early Monday morning.  A strong cold front that extended southwest from this low, swept across the local region early Monday morning.Lines of thunderstorms formed in the moist unstable airmass ahead of the cold front.  These storms produced numerous wind gusts from 58 MPH in East Orange of Essex County to 65 MPH at both Garwood and Berkeley Heights in Union County.Thunderstorms also caused torrential rainfall.  The Ramapo River at Mahwah crested at 8.10 feet, just above its' 8.00 foot flood stage.  Small stream, street, and poor drainage flooding was widespread over Union, Passaic, and Bergen Counties.Occasional rain began across the region during Saturday afternoon, November 30th.  Here are selected rainfall event totals:For Passaic County:  from 1.82 inches at Wanaque to 4.51 inches at Ringwood.For Bergen County:  from 2.00 inches at Franklin Lakes to 4.38 inches at Saddle River.For Essex County:  from 1.83 inches at Newark Airport to 3.60 inches at Verona.For Hudson County:  from 1.59 inches at Jersey City to 2.20 inches at Harrison.For Union County:  from 2.15 inches at Canoe Brook to 2.61 inches at Elizabeth.
A severe thunderstorm downed power lines and uprooted trees in Pennsville Township.  An entire carport was lifted and flipped over within a mobile home park.  No injuries were reported.

As Tropical Storm Bertha moved northeast, passing east of Atlantic City around 11 am and off the Central New Jersey coast by early afternoon, it produced torrential rain across Northeast New Jersey.Torrential rain caused flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas, streams, and rivers across the area.   Numerous rivers and small streams rose out of their banks and widespread roadway closures occurred due to flooding.  At its' peak, rainfall rates were up to 2 inches per hour.Across Bergen County, the following streams and rivers rose above flood stage:Hohokus Brook at Hohokus from 8:15 am to 9:45 am EST.  Flood stage is 3.0 feet.  The crest stage was 3.05 feet at 9:15 am.The Saddle River at Lodi from 12:15 pm to 10:00 pm EST.  Flood stage is  5.0 feet.   The crest stage was 5.87 feet at 5:00 pm.The Ramapo River at Mahwah from 5:30 pm on July 13th through 9:15 am EST on July 14th.  Flood  stage is  8.0 feet.  The crest stage was  8.47 feet on July 14th at 12:45 am.Here are selected rainfall amounts:For Passaic County:  from 2.58 inches at Little Falls to 4.85 inches at West Milford.For Bergen County:  from 2.17 inches at River Vale to 3.82 inches at Ridgewood.For Essex County:  from 2.36 inches at Newark Airport to 2.96 inches at Essex Falls.For  Hudson County:  2.32 inches was measured at Harrison.For Union County:  from 2.43 inches at Elizabeth to 2.79 inches at Canoe Brook.Wind gusts were measured from 31 MPH at Caldwell Airport in Essex County to 41 MPH at Harrison in Hudson County.  Newark Airport reported a wind gust of 40 MPH.

Snowfall amounts ranged from a trace along the North Jersey Coast to 2.0 inches at Ringwood.

A slow moving low pressure system moved from Williamsburg, Virginia the morning of the 13th to about 100 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 14th and then drifted southeast to about 300 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 15th.  This low combined with a high pressure system anchored over Quebec Province Canada to produce heavy rain and an upstream flow for several days.  This was exacerbated by the astronomical spring tides with the new moon.  Moderate tidal flooding resulted the afternoon of the 13th with minor tidal flooding persisted mainly through the next two high tide cycles.  The tide on the Delaware River at Pier 9 in Philadelphia reached 9.2 feet above mean low water at 345 p.m. EST on the 13th and 8.8 feet above mean low water at 430 p.m. EST on the 14th.  Tidal departures reached four feet above normal (But were around 2 feet at the time of high tide.).  Admiral Wilson Boulevard near the Cooper River in Camden was closed.  There was also some low-lying area flooding along the Delaware River through the 14th.

December 1996 was the wettest December on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The statewide average of 7.9 inches surpassed the previous record of 7.29 inches set in 1973.  Precipitation amounts ranged from about 3.5 inches above normal in Middlesex County to 5.7 inches above normal in Warren County.  A new December precipitation record was set at nearby Philadelphia International Airport where 8.48 inches fell.  December 1996 was the second wettest on record at the Atlantic City International Airport (in Pomona) where 7.23 inches of precipitation fell.  December 1996 was the 8th wettest on record at the Marina in Atlantic City as 6.87 inches of rain fell.  County average rainfall totals ranged from 7.4 inches in Middlesex County to 9.2 inches in Hunterdon, Morris and Warren Counties.

According to state climatological records, the year 1996 was the wettest on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The state-wide average was 59.97 inches, about 14.5 inches above normal.  The previous record was 58.85 inches set in 1975.   Precipitation amounts on a county average ranged from 56.5 inches in Cumberland County to 65.3 inches in Morris County.  The wettest county was  Bergen where a county weighed average of 70.7 inches fell. It was the wettest year on record at Lambertville (Hunterdon County) as 69.82 inches of precipitation fell.  It was also the wettest year on record in Philadelphia as 56.47 inches of precipitation fell.  Other yearly totals included 64.53 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 63.21 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 61.52 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County/3rd wettest), 60.53 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 57.58 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 56.38 inches in Cape May (3rd wettest) and 50.46 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona.  On a county average, the yearly precipitation total ranged from 12 to 21 inches above normal (It was higher in Northeast New Jersey).

A low pressure system over the Ohio Valley early Sunday morning on December 1st, intensified as it moved northeast across Eastern Canada during early Monday morning.  A strong cold front that extended southwest from this low, swept across the local region early Monday morning.Lines of thunderstorms formed in the moist unstable airmass ahead of the cold front.  These storms produced numerous wind gusts from 58 MPH in East Orange of Essex County to 65 MPH at both Garwood and Berkeley Heights in Union County.Thunderstorms also caused torrential rainfall.  The Ramapo River at Mahwah crested at 8.10 feet, just above its' 8.00 foot flood stage.  Small stream, street, and poor drainage flooding was widespread over Union, Passaic, and Bergen Counties.Occasional rain began across the region during Saturday afternoon, November 30th.  Here are selected rainfall event totals:For Passaic County:  from 1.82 inches at Wanaque to 4.51 inches at Ringwood.For Bergen County:  from 2.00 inches at Franklin Lakes to 4.38 inches at Saddle River.For Essex County:  from 1.83 inches at Newark Airport to 3.60 inches at Verona.For Hudson County:  from 1.59 inches at Jersey City to 2.20 inches at Harrison.For Union County:  from 2.15 inches at Canoe Brook to 2.61 inches at Elizabeth.

A severe thunderstorm caused straight line wind damage in about a 1.5 mile long area within the township.  Six homes were severely damaged and about 15 others suffered minor damage mainly from trees on homes.  About 20 townships roads were closed because of fallen trees.  Some of the damaged trees were 80 years old.  No injuries were reported.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Josephine moved rapidly northeast off the Atlantic Coast during Tuesday, October 8th. Most peak wind gusts were measured from 40 to 50 mph.  At Newark Airport, a peak wind gust of 47 mph was recorded.

An F0 tornado briefly touched down in East Brunswick and damaged three buildings in "The Courts" Condominium Complex.  An eight foot by twenty-five foot section of a roof was pulled from one building.  The chimney was knocked down from another building and damaged a third.  About 45 families were made homeless by the damage.  Elsewhere in East Brunswick the thunderstorm produced damaging wind gusts at the Lawrence Brook School.  The winds damaged the school's roof and shattered windows.  Some roof damage also occurred at the Miracle Mall.  Several trees were also uprooted throughout the township.  Tornado damage was estimated at $10,000.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina the afternoon of the 5th.  As it quickly intensified and moved northeast (It passed just east of the New Jersey coast the morning of the 6th.), heavy rain spread across New Jersey after midnight on the 6th.  Storm totals averaged around 1.50 inches across the area and produced some urban and poor drainage flooding.  This was the second soaking precipitation event within the week and month.  Rainfall amounts were less than one inch farther southeast in the state.  The rain changed briefly to snow before ending across western parts of the state.  Precipitation totals included 1.74 inches in Reigelsville (Hunterdon County), 1.66 inches in Freehold (Monmouth County), 1.62 inches at the McGuire AFB, 1.5 inches in Seabrook (Cumberland County), 1.48 inches in Wertsville (Hunterdon County), 1.44 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 1.34 inches in Washington's Crossing (Mercer County), 1.29 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County) and 1.24 inches in Belvidere (Warren County).

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina the afternoon of the 5th.  As it quickly intensified and moved northeast (It passed just east of the New Jersey coast the morning of the 6th.), heavy rain spread across Morris County after midnight on the 6th.Storm totals averaged between 1.00 and 1.50 inches across the county.  This heavy rain combined with the recent heavy rain on the 1st and 2nd was enough to push the Passaic River at Pine Brook above flood stage again.  The river was above its estimated 19 foot flood stage from 730 a.m. EST on the 6th through 6 a.m. EST on the 11th.  The river crested at 19.5 feet at 815 p.m. EST on the 8th.  A second shot of heavy rain during the afternoon and evening of the 7th averaged about an inch in Morris County.  This caused additional rivers to flood.  The Passaic River at Chatham was above its 6 foot flood stage from 745 p.m. EST on the 7th to midnight EST on the 8th.  It crested at 6.1 feet at 9 p.m. EST.  The Rockaway River below the Boonton Reservoir reached its 5 foot flood stage at 530 a.m. EST on the 8th, crested at 5.2 feet at 1215 p.m. EST on the 8th and was within bankfull again at 530 a.m. EST on the 9th.  No serious injuries or damage were reported.

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina and Georgia the morning of the 7th and moved quickly northeast passing over the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon of the 7th, just east of the New Jersey during the evening of the 7th and near Portland Maine on the morning of the 8th.  For the third time this month at least an inch of rain (heavy at times during the late afternoon and early evening) fell across most of Western New Jersey.  Some urban and poor drainage flooding occurred,  but no serious injuries or damage were reported.  Storm totals included 1.58 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.50 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 1.37 inches in Millville (Cumberland County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 1.33 inches within Atlantic City, 1.28 inches in Verga (Gloucester County), 1.20 inches in Atsion (Burlington County), 1.19 inches in Cherry Hill (Camden County) and Mount Holly (Burlington County) and 1.18 inches in Somerdale (Camden County).

Thunderstorms produced high winds and torrential rain.High winds knocked down trees and power lines at Scotch Plains and Elizabeth in Union County.   In addition, two cars were damaged on 31st Street and Ave. C in Jersey City when downed trees fell on them.Torrential rain caused flash flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas, including many roads.  In particular, major flooding was reported along Routes 1 and 9 in Hudson County.

A slow moving low pressure system moved from the central Ohio Valley the morning of the 12th to Williamsburg, Virginia the morning of the 13th, to about 100 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 14th, then drifted southeast and was about 300 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 15th.  This produced about 48 hours of continuous rain from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th.  Rain became heavy at times during the night of the 13th through the morning of the 14th.  Rain mixed with snow at times during the morning of the 14th over some of the higher terrain of Northwest New Jersey.  Accumulation averaged around two inches in Wantage, Newton and Vernon.  Storm totals averaged between one and four inches with the lowest amounts in Sussex County and the highest amounts in the southwest part of the state.   Since the rain was spread over an extended period of time, flooding was mainly confined to urban and poor drainage areas.  Basement flooding did occur, with the highest reports in Burlington County.  The heavy rain also damaged a bridge over the Cranbury Creek in Plainsboro (Middlesex County).  Precipitation totals included 4.28 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County),  3.89 inches in Verga (Gloucester County), 2.61 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), 2.49 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.00 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.93 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 1.71 inches within Atlantic City, 1.58 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.37 inches in Newton (Sussex County).

A low pressure system developed over South Carolina and Georgia the morning of the 7th and moved quickly northeast passing over the Delmarva Peninsula during the late afternoon of the 7th, just east of the New Jersey coast during the evening of the 7th and near Portland, Maine on the morning of the 8th.  The onshore flow accompanying the low pressure system kept the tide from receding overnight and the ensuing high tide the morning of the 8th was about 2 feet above normal south of Manasquan Inlet.  The high tide at Atlantic City reached 6.6 feet above mean low water.  Tides were about 2.5 feet above normal along Coastal Monmouth County.  Only minor tidal flooding was reported along New Jersey's coast.

Heavy rain associated with a storm system that passed just east of New Jersey during the evening of the 7th caused flooding along some of the smaller streams in Hunterdon County. A slow moving cold front, coupled with a low pressure system that developed over West Virginia on the 1st and moved just northwest of New Jersey on the 2nd brought rain to the area from the 1st through the morning of the 2nd.  The rain was the heaviest during the early morning of the 2nd and caused some urban and poor drainage flooding.  River flooding was mainly confined to the Passaic and Raritan Basins.  Storm totals averaged between 1.5 and 2.5 inches with higher amounts in the northwest part of the state and lower amounts along the southeast shore.  Storm totals included 3.17 inches in Sparta (Sussex County), 2.89 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.53 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 2.19 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 1.76 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County), 1.75 inches in Brick Township (Ocean County), 1.68 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.61 inches in Millville (Cumberland County), 1.6 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County) and 1.06 inches within Atlantic City.

On Friday October 18th, a strong low pressure system developed on a cold front over the DelMarVa Peninsula.  With a high pressure system in place across Northern New England, the low intensified as it moved slowly off the Southern New Jersey Coast during the 19th.The difference in pressures caused strong and gusty east winds, which transported abundant moisture from the Atlantic Ocean across the region.  Heavy flood producing rains resulted with amounts up to 8 inches.  Although the rain ended by midnight on the 20th, river flooding extended until almost midnight on the 21st.Heavy rainfall produced serious flooding of many rivers and small streams as well as serious widespread street and poor drainage flooding.The Rahway River in Springfield (Union County) reached its' second highest level of record.  In Cranford, downstream of the Springfield gage, 100 homes were evacuated, and in Rahway, further downstream, 75 homes and one high rise apartment were evacuated due to rising flood waters.  Early reports from the Union County Emergency Management Office estimate $4 million dollars in damage to private homeowners and $300,000 in township damages.Five gaged rivers exceeded their flood stages:  the Saddle River at Lodi, the Ramapo River at Mahwah, the Rahway River at Springfield, Hohokus Brook at Hohokus, and the Third River at Bloomfield.In Passaic County, one fatality occurred in Paterson when a 47 year old man lost control of his car while driving on rain-slicken roads and crashed into a bus.  Significant flooding was observed in Wayne, Little Falls, Clifton, and Paterson.In Essex County, the towns of Millburn, Short Hills, and Fairfield were hit hard.  In Fairfield (like in so many other areas), several roads were forced to close due to flooding as the Passaic River crested.  Commerce Road was under two feet of water leaving several vehicles stuck.  Sections of Horseneck Road and Bloomfield Ave. were also impassable.  In Roseland, the Mayor estimated that the cost to repair erosion damage to the culverts of Canoe and Fullerton Brooks was up to $5 million dollars.In Hudson County, low-lying roads across the entire county were shut by several feet of water including:  Lincoln Highway and Route 440 in Jersey City and Kearny; Meadowlands Parkway and Secaucus Road in Secaucus; Observer Highway in Hoboken; and West Side Ave. in North Bergen.A presidential disaster was declared for people in Union and Hudson Counties.Here are selected rainfall amounts for:Union County (5 to 8 inches):  from 5.15 inches at New Providence to 7.99 inches at Mountainside.Essex County (4 to 8 inches):  from 4.12 inches at Newark Airport (a new daily record) to 7.68 inches in Verona.Hudson County (4 to 5 inches):  from 4.46 inches at Harrison to 5.04 inches at Jersey City.Passaic County (3 to 6 inches):  from 3.25 inches at Greenwood Lake to 6.36 inches at Wayne.Bergen County (3 to 5 inches):  from 3.50 inches at Ramsey to 5.26 inches at Lodi.

In Roseland, the Mayor estimated that the cost to repair erosion damage to the culverts of Canoe and Fullerton Brooks was up to $5 million dollars.In Hudson County, low-lying roads across the entire county were shut by several feet of water including:  Lincoln Highway and Route 440 in Jersey City and Kearny; Meadowlands Parkway and Secaucus Road in Secaucus; Observer Highway in Hoboken; and West Side Ave. in North Bergen.A presidential disaster was declared for people in Union and Hudson Counties.Here are selected rainfall amounts for:Union County (5 to 8 inches):  from 5.15 inches at New Providence to 7.99 inches at Mountainside.Essex County (4 to 8 inches):  from 4.12 inches at Newark Airport (a new daily record) to 7.68 inches in Verona.Hudson County (4 to 5 inches):  from 4.46 inches at Harrison to 5.04 inches at Jersey City.Passaic County (3 to 6 inches):  from 3.25 inches at Greenwood Lake to 6.36 inches at Wayne.Bergen County (3 to 5 inches):  from 3.50 inches at Ramsey to 5.26 inches at Lodi.

On Friday October 18th, a strong low pressure system developed on a cold front over the DelMarVa Peninsula.  With a high pressure system in place across Northern New England, the low intensified as it moved slowly off the Southern New Jersey Coast during the 19th.The difference in pressures caused strong and gusty east winds, which transported abundant moisture from the Atlantic Ocean across the region.  Heavy flood producing rains resulted with amounts up to 8 inches.  Although the rain ended by midnight on the 20th, river flooding extended until almost midnight on the 21st.Heavy rainfall produced serious flooding of many rivers and small streams as well as serious widespread street and poor drainage flooding.The Rahway River in Springfield (Union County) reached its' second highest level of record.  In Cranford, downstream of the Springfield gage, 100 homes were evacuated, and in Rahway, further downstream, 75 homes and one high rise apartment were evacuated due to rising flood waters.  Early reports from the Union County Emergency Management Office estimate $4 million dollars in damage to private homeowners and $300,000 in township damages.Five gaged rivers exceeded their flood stages:  the Saddle River at Lodi, the Ramapo River at Mahwah, the Rahway River at Springfield, Hohokus Brook at Hohokus, and the Third River at Bloomfield.In Passaic County, one fatality occurred in Paterson when a 47 year old man lost control of his car while driving on rain-slicken roads and crashed into a bus.  Significant flooding was observed in Wayne, Little Falls, Clifton, and Paterson.In Essex County, the towns of Millburn, Short Hills, and Fairfield were hit hard.  In Fairfield (like in so many other areas), several roads were forced to close due to flooding as the Passaic River crested.  Commerce Road was under two feet of water leaving several vehicles stuck.  Sections of Horseneck Road and Bloomfield Ave. were also impassable.  In Roseland, the Mayor estimated that the cost to repair erosion damage to the culverts of Canoe and Fullerton Brooks was up to $5 million dollars.In Hudson County, low-lying roads across the entire county were shut by several feet of water including:  Lincoln Highway and Route 440 in Jersey City and Kearny; Meadowlands Parkway and Secaucus Road in Secaucus; Observer Highway in Hoboken; and West Side Ave. in North Bergen.A presidential disaster was declared for people in Union and Hudson Counties.Here are selected rainfall amounts for:Union County (5 to 8 inches):  from 5.15 inches at New Providence to 7.99 inches at Mountainside.Essex County (4 to 8 inches):  from 4.12 inches at Newark Airport (a new daily record) to 7.68 inches in Verona.Hudson County (4 to 5 inches):  from 4.46 inches at Harrison to 5.04 inches at Jersey City.Passaic County (3 to 6 inches):  from 3.25 inches at Greenwood Lake to 6.36 inches at Wayne.Bergen County (3 to 5 inches):  from 3.50 inches at Ramsey to 5.26 inches at Lodi.

The strong onshore winds which coincided with the late afternoon and early evening high tide caused minor to locally moderate tidal flooding.  Tidal departures at high tide averaged between 2.5 and 3.0 feet above normal. In western Atlantic City three of the four lanes of the Black Horse Pike (U.S. Routes 40/322) were closed.  In Ocean County within Little Egg Harbor Township parts of Osburn Island's roads were flooded.  In Point Pleasant Beach New Jersey State Route 35 South and Broadway were flooded.  In Monmouth County, the back roads in Sea Bright were flooded near the Shrewsbury River.  Flooding was reported in Manasquan near the 4th Avenue Bridge.  Minor flooding was reported in Keansburg.The high tide at Atlantic City reached 7.3 feet above mean low water, a departure of 3.1 feet above normal.  High tides at Cape May were 7.8 feet (2.6 feet above normal) above mean low water and at Sandy Hook at 6.9 feet (2.4 feet above normal) above mean low water.  The high tide at North Widlwood reached 8.1 feet above mean low water.  No serious injuries were reported.

Rain, heavy at times, especially during the morning of the 19th caused widespread urban flooding and the flash flooding of several streams within Mercer County.  The rain was over by early afternoon, but flooding along the Assunpink Creek lasted through the morning of the 20th.  Flood waters from the Assunpink Creek reached the stoops of houses on two blocks in Trenton.  The worst flooding was along Mulberry Street.  The road was still closed as of 9 p.m. EDT on the 19th.  The Princeton Pike and United States Route 1 were badly flooded in Trenton and several roads were also closed in Ewing.  In Princeton, Quaker Road had four feet of water on it.The Assunpink Creek in Trenton crested at 10.4 feet at 645 p.m. EDT on the 19th.  Flood stage is 7 feet.  Storm precipitation totals included 4.48 inches in Washington's Crossing, 4.2 inches in Princeton, 3.97 inches in Hightstown, 3.8 inches at the University of New Jersey, 3.6 inches in Windsor and 3.09 inches in Hamilton Square.  No serious injuries were reported.

High winds associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Josephine caused wind damage along coastal New Jersey.  Wind gusts reached 70 mph at the Atlantic City Beach Patrol and 65 mph in Seaside Park. In Cape May County the high winds pulled down power lines. They also tore part of a roof away in Cape May City.  In Atlantic County, trees were uprooted in Margate and Brigantine.  A house in Brigantine suffered roof damage.  About 31,000 customers lost power.  The hardest hit townships were Northfield and Egg Harbor. In Ocean County, the hardest hit communities were Berkley Township, Brick, Point Pleasant, Seaside Heights and Tom's River.  About 14,000 homes lost power.  In Brick, about 10 trees were uprooted, a few fell on homes and vehicles.  There were numerous calls of downed trees and power lines in Dover Township.  In Seaside Heights, a hotel under construction had pieces of its roof blow away.  Awnings and street signs were blown away.  In Monmouth County, downed trees and wires were reported in Spring Lake.  No serious injuries were reported.Other peak wind gusts included 61 mph in Harvey Cedars, 60 mph at the Atlantic City Marina, 54 mph in Cape May, 52 mph in Brigantine, 49 mph in Tom's River, 46 mph in Longport and 43 mph in Belmar.

According to state climatological records, the year 1996 was the wettest on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The state-wide average was 59.97 inches, about 14.5 inches above normal.  The previous record was 58.85 inches set in 1975.   Precipitation amounts on a county average ranged from 56.5 inches in Cumberland County to 65.3 inches in Morris County.  The wettest county was  Bergen where a county weighed average of 70.7 inches fell. It was the wettest year on record at Lambertville (Hunterdon County) as 69.82 inches of precipitation fell.  It was also the wettest year on record in Philadelphia as 56.47 inches of precipitation fell.  Other yearly totals included 64.53 inches in Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 63.21 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 61.52 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County/3rd wettest), 60.53 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 57.58 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County), 56.38 inches in Cape May (3rd wettest) and 50.46 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona.  On a county average, the yearly precipitation total ranged from 12 to 21 inches above normal (It was higher in Northeast New Jersey).

A strong low pressure system developed just off the DELMARVA Coast early Friday morning and moved slowly northeast. A slow moving cold front, coupled with a low pressure system that developed over West Virginia on the 1st and moved just northwest of New Jersey on the 2nd brought rain to the area from the 1st through the morning of the 2nd.  The rain was the heaviest during the early morning of the 2nd and caused some urban and poor drainage flooding.  River flooding was mainly confined to the Passaic and Raritan Basins. A low pressure system over the Ohio Valley early Sunday morning on December 1st, intensified as it moved northeast across Eastern Canada during early Monday morning.  A strong cold front that extended southwest from this low, swept across the local region early Monday morning.Lines of thunderstorms formed in the moist unstable airmass ahead of the cold front.  These storms produced numerous wind gusts from 58 MPH in East Orange of Essex County to 65 MPH at both Garwood and Berkeley Heights in Union County.Thunderstorms also caused torrential rainfall.  The Ramapo River at Mahwah crested at 8.10 feet, just above its' 8.00 foot flood stage.  Small stream, street, and poor drainage flooding was widespread over Union, Passaic, and Bergen Counties.Occasional rain began across the region during Saturday afternoon, November 30th.  Here are selected rainfall event totals:For Passaic County:  from 1.82 inches at Wanaque to 4.51 inches at Ringwood.For Bergen County:  from 2.00 inches at Franklin Lakes to 4.38 inches at Saddle River.For Essex County:  from 1.83 inches at Newark Airport to 3.60 inches at Verona.For Hudson County:  from 1.59 inches at Jersey City to 2.20 inches at Harrison.For Union County:  from 2.15 inches at Canoe Brook to 2.61 inches at Elizabeth.

Heavy rain the night of the 13th associated with a low pressure system near Delaware and the saturated soil that preceded this low pressure system caused flooding along the Cooper River in Camden County.  Flooding was aided by tidal flooding near the mouth of the Cooper River that slowed the flow of water downstream.  The Cooper River at Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 1045 p.m. EST on the 13th through 445 p.m. EST on the 14th.  It crested at 3.14 feet at 545 a.m. EST on the 14th.  This heavy rain was part of a larger and almost continuous period of rain that lasted for 48 hours from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th.  Storm totals included 4.28 inches in Pennsauken, 3.15 inches in Somerdale and 3.12 inches in Cherry Hill.

Heavy rain the night of the 13th associated with a low pressure system near Delaware and the saturated soil that preceded this low pressure system caused river flooding in Hunterdon, Morris and Somerset Counties.  This heavy rain was part of a larger and almost continuous period of rain that lasted about 48 hours from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th. In Hunterdon County, the South Branch of the Raritan River at Stanton was above its 8 foot flood stage from 545 a.m through 915 a.m. EST on the 14th.  It crested at 8.13 feet at 715 a.m. EST on the 14th.  Storm totals included 3.88 inches in Riegelsville and 3.2 inches in Wertsville.In Morris County, the Rockaway River below the Boonton Reservoir was barely above its 5 foot flood stage from 1 a.m. through 8 a.m. EST on the 15th.  It crested at 5.02 feet at 245 a.m. EST on the 15th.  The Passaic River at Pine Brook was barely above its estimated flood stage of 19 feet from 1245 a.m. EST on the 15th through 1145 a.m. EST on the 16th.  It crested at 19.04 feet at 730 p.m. EST on the 15th.  Storm totals included 2.22 inches in Long Valley and 1.76 inches in Boonton. In Somerset County, the Raritan River at Manville reached its 12 foot flood stage at 9 a.m. EST on the 14th, crested at 14.8 feet at 6 p.m. EST on the 14th and was within bankfull at 3 a.m. EST on the 15th.  At Bound Brook, the Raritan River was above its 26 foot flood stage from 2 p.m. EST on the 14th through 8 a.m. EST on the 15th.  It crested at 28.4 feet at 930 p.m. EST on the 14th.  The Millstone River at Blackwell Mills reached its 9 foot flood stage at 130 p.m. EST on the 14th, crested at 10.8 feet at 1030 p.m. EST on the 14th and was within its banks again at 2 p.m. EST on the 15th.  Hillsborough was hardest hit with five roadways and one bridge closed due to flooding.  Three other roads were closed due to flooding in Branchburg, Bridgewater, Franklin and Montgomery.  Storm totals included 1.87 inches in Pottersville.

The remnants of Pacific Hurricane Fausto moved from Mexico northeast and reformed into a large coastal storm off the DelMarVa Coast on Tuesday morning, September 17th.  It moved slowly northeast  and passed east of Cape Cod MA during Wednesday afternoon.This storm produced heavy rain and strong gusty winds.Rainfall amounts ranged from 2 to 3 inches over Northeast New Jersey:  from 1.96 inches at Boonton to 2.92 inches at Canoe Brook.  Newark Airport recorded 2.57 inches.Winds that gusted from 40 to 50 MPH during Tuesday, September 17th caused tree limbs and branches  to fall.  High winds also caused scattered power outages.

A slow moving low pressure system moved from the central Ohio Valley the morning of the 12th to Williamsburg, Virginia the morning of the 13th, to about 100 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 14th, then drifted southeast and was about 300 miles southeast of Atlantic City the morning of the 15th.  This produced about 48 hours of continuous rain from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th.  Rain became heavy at times during the night of the 13th through the morning of the 14th.  Rain mixed with snow at times during the morning of the 14th over some of the higher terrain of Northwest New Jersey.  Accumulation averaged around two inches in Wantage, Newton and Vernon.  Storm totals averaged between one and four inches with the lowest amounts in Sussex County and the highest amounts in the southwest part of the state.   Since the rain was spread over an extended period of time, flooding was mainly confined to urban and poor drainage areas.  Basement flooding did occur, with the highest reports in Burlington County.  The heavy rain also damaged a bridge over the Cranbury Creek in Plainsboro (Middlesex County).  Precipitation totals included 4.28 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County),  3.89 inches in Verga (Gloucester County), 2.61 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County), 2.49 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County), 2.00 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 1.93 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 1.71 inches within Atlantic City, 1.58 inches in New Brunswick (Middlesex County) and 1.37 inches in Newton (Sussex County).

December 1996 was the wettest December on record (1895-present) for New Jersey.  The statewide average of 7.9 inches surpassed the previous record of 7.29 inches set in 1973.  Precipitation amounts ranged from about 3.5 inches above normal in Middlesex County to 5.7 inches above normal in Warren County.  A new December precipitation record was set at nearby Philadelphia International Airport where 8.48 inches fell.  December 1996 was the second wettest on record at the Atlantic City International Airport (in Pomona) where 7.23 inches of precipitation fell.  December 1996 was the 8th wettest on record at the Marina in Atlantic City as 6.87 inches of rain fell.  County average rainfall totals ranged from 7.4 inches in Middlesex County to 9.2 inches in Hunterdon, Morris and Warren Counties.

Heavy rain the night of the 13th associated with a low pressure system near Delaware and the saturated grounds that preceded the rain caused flooding along the Assunpink Creek in Mercer County. The creek was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1045 p.m. EST on the 13th through 545 p.m. EST on the 14th.  It crested at 8.4 feet at 1015 a.m. EST on the 14th.  Flooding along the Assunpink forced the closure of Mulberry Street.  Heavy rain also forced the closure of several heavily traveled roads in Princeton and Hamilton.  Many houses in the county needed their basements pumped.  The heavy rain was part of a 48 hour stretch of nearly continuous rain from the evening of the 12th through the evening of the 14th.  Storm totals included 3.65 inches in Washington's Crossing, 1.78 inches in Princeton and 1.5 inches in Hightstown.

A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail in Florence and golf ball size hail in Chesterfield.  It also caused wind damage in a four block wide area in Florence from 5th to 8th Streets.  Several trees were uprooted, one crushed a Cadillac.  A flag was literally ripped from a flagpole.  No injuries were reported.
A severe thunderstorm tore down trees and wires in Jackson.
A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Freehold.  Wind damage was also reported in Howell Township.
In addition to nickel size hail in Marlton, there were numerous reports of marble size hail from Mount Holly southwest through Evesham Township.

Thunderstorms moved over the same locations in Northwestern Burlington County and considerable urban and poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of the smaller streams in the area.  Hardest hit was Hanover Township.  Storm totals included 3.15 inches at McGurie Air Force Base and 2.49 inches in Mount Holly.  Most of this fell within two hours between 6 p.m. and 8 p.m. EDT.

A 55-year-old man was critically injured when struck by lightning while working on a car in his driveway in Andover. He suffered burns below his knees.  It had rained and lightning apparently struck the wet ground about 15 feet from where he was working.  It formed a trench in the dirt and blew holes in the concrete driveway.  The heat from the lightning was so intense, it melted some of the cars' tires parked in the driveway.

A low pressure system that developed along the Gulf Coast States Monday morning the 12th moved northeast and passed through the Delmarva Peninsula the morning of the 13th.  It tapped the abundant moisture over the South and consequently produced heavy rain across Central and Southern New Jersey.  Rainfall totals averaged between 1 and 2 inches across Central New Jersey and 2.5 to 4 inches across Southern New Jersey.  This occurred over about a 24 hour span thus flooding was limited to poor drainage locations.  No significant  stream flooding was reported.  Storm totals included 3.63 inches in Estell Manor, 3.62 inches in Margate, 3.3 inches in Atlantic City, 2.8 inches in Harvey Cedars, 2.76 inches in Verga, 2.54 inches in Millville, 2.25 inches in Cherry Hill, 1.63 inches in Hazlet, 1.52 inches in Washington Crossing and 1.41 inches in Iselin.
A severe thunderstorm uprooted several trees in Bernardsville.  In nearby Bernards Township three blocks were closed because of fallen trees.
A severe thunderstorm tore down trees in Neshanic and Hillsborough.  No serious injuries or property damage were reported.

Heavy rain, associated with the remnants of Pacific Hurricane Fausto caused considerable flooding on Long Beach Island.  At Harvey Cedars, 6.07 inches of rain fell.  Hardest hit were Surf City and Ship Bottom.  In Ship Bottom, 15 to 20 cars became stranded within the flood waters.  The eastbound causeway lanes on 8th and 9th Streets (access to the bridge) were closed.  Barnegat Avenue was also closed.  Both elementary schools on the island were closed for the day.  No serious injuries were reported.  Heavy rain also fell farther south along the New Jersey shore.  Storm totals included 4 inches in Stratmere and 2.97 inches in Atlantic City.  Flooding though was not as widespread and only a few roads were closed.  The heavy rain did wash out a section of the eastbound roadside of New Jersey State Route 147 in the Wildwoods.

High winds developed during the evening of the 19th as a low pressure system intensified across the central Appalachians and its associated occluded front moved through the region.  The winds were strong enough to down trees, power lines and large limbs throughout the state.About 91,000 customers throughout New Jersey lost power because of the high winds taking down trees and power lines.  Most lost power between 6 p.m. and 730 p.m. with power restored by 11 p.m.  But, a few isolated locations had no power for up to 24 hours.  The worst damage occurred at 715 p.m. in East Windsor when part of the roof of the Shalom Torah Academy's synagogue and grade school blew away. Warren and Hunterdon Counties had the most widespread damage in the state with damage reported in the following communities: Alpha, Annandale, Califon, Great Meadows, Hampton, Milford, Port Murray, Stockton and Washington.  In Phillipsburg the wind was strong enough to bend a pole holding a sign at Stateline Ford.  The sign damaged three cars.  In Mercer County Princeton reported numerous fallen trees on roads.  Hamilton and Hopewell Valley were also hard hit.  In Burlington County 13 reports of downed trees were received from Medford, Chesterfield and Springfield.  Also, the roof awning of a porch collapsed.  In Middlesex County, in North Brunswick nine trees were uprooted and damaged 2 cars.  A Woodbridge driver escaped injury when a utility pole broke and shattered the car's windshield.  Recorded wind gusts reached 58 mph in Hazlet and 62 mph in Seaside Park.
A low pressure system moved north along the Atlantic Coast and produced heavy snow across the area.  The snow became heavy after midnight.  Accumulations ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

A low pressure system formed off the South Carolina Coastal waters on the morning of the 1st.  It moved north during the next two days and was about 200 miles east of Cape Hatteras at dawn on the 2nd and about 150 miles east of Atlantic City during the afternoon.  Its storm track was far enough offshore to place the axis of heavy snow across New Jersey.  Very little snow fell farther west.Snow began just after midnight on the 2nd and fell at a heavy clip almost from the start.  The heavy snow was over by noon.  Accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were fairly common throughout the southern half of the state.  Around 6 inches of snow fell in Mercer, Middlesex and Monmouth Counties; 4 to 5 inches in Morris, Hunterdon and Somerset Counties and 2 to 4 inches across Warren and Sussex Counties.  Some representative accumulations included 9 inches at McGuire AFB and Tabernacle, 8 inches in Mount Holly, Toms River and Washington Township (Gloucester County), 7 inches in Marlboro and Cherry Hill and 6 inches in Pilesgrove, Iselin, Atlantic City and Cape May City.Because this snow fell on a Saturday morning,  the number of accidents was held down.  There were two separate crashes involving buses with snow as a contributing factor.  In Old Bridge Township at 520 a.m., a 65-year-old man passsenger was killed on a Gray Line Tour Bus as it slid and overturned on the Garden State Parkway.  Thirty-four other people were injured.  The parkway was closed for seven hours between the Asbury Park and Raritan Toll Plazas.  At 8 a.m. in Hammonton a Greyhound Bus coming from Atlantic City collided with a Chevrolet Monte Carlo that was spinning out of control on the Atlantic City Expressway.  Forty-six passengers were injured.

The coldest air mass of the winter season moved in after the snow storm of the 2nd and 3rd.  Except for the immediate coast and along parts of the Delaware River and Bay, most locations had low temperatures below zero on the 5th and 6th.  A record low temperature of -8F was set at Atlantic City International Airport in Pomona on the 5th.  In central New Jersey alone, the Automobile Association of America responded to over 900 calls of dead batteries the morning of the 5th.  About 100 residents of the Southern Ocean Nursing and Rehabilitation Center in Manahawkin were evacuated on the 6th after a frozen pipe burst within the center's sprinkler system.  They returned that evening.Low temperatures the morning of the 5th included -17F in Newton, -16F in Hainesville, -10F in Estelle Manor and New Lisbon, -8F in Hightstown and Blackwell Mills, -6F in Boonton, zero in Mount Holly, 2F in Trenton, 4F within Atlantic City and 7F in Sandy Hook. Low temperatures the morning of the 6th were about the same and they included -14F in Newton, -13F in Sussex and East Millstone, -8F in Belvidere, -4F in New Brunswick, -3F at Atlantic City International Airport a new record, -2F in Mount Laurel and Somerdale and 5F within Atlantic City.  No serious cold related injuries were reported.
